name: Donald Trump
summary: Donald Trump's character and pattern of behavior are marked by a history of dishonesty, racism, and self-serving actions. He has repeatedly made false claims, such as exaggerating his academic achievements and business success, and has been involved in numerous lawsuits and bankruptcies. Trump has also demonstrated a willingness to engage in discriminatory behavior, including countersuing the U.S. government over charges of racial discrimination in his properties and implementing policies that target marginalized groups, such as Muslims and immigrants. Additionally, Trump has shown a disregard for the law and ethical standards, including failing to release his tax returns, profiting from his businesses while in office, and being involved in multiple cases of fraud and abuse of power.

entries.fact: Trump claimed to be top of his class with the highest grades possible at Wharton, but Wharton's published academic honors and dean's list do not include his name.

Donald Trump countersued the U.S. government for $100 million over charges that his properties had discriminated against Black applicants and tenants, with the help of Roy Cohn. The case was settled with a consent decree agreeing to desegregate, which the Trumps ended up in court for violating four years later.
Trump paid $70 million to Holiday Corporation to take sole control of Harrah's at Trump Plaza, which was unprofitable.

Donald Trump filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection for six of his businesses, including the Plaza Hotel in Manhattan and the casinos in Atlantic City, New Jersey. Trump filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection for the Trump Taj Mahal. Trump defaulted on his loans for the Trump Shuttle airline, and ownership passed to the banks.

Donald Trump formed a shell company to pay vendors for his rental units, then billed those services and supplies to Trump Management with significant markups, inflating rents and transferring assets from his father to his children and nephew. Trump's casinos, including Trump Taj Mahal and Trump Castle, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection.

Donald Trump defaulted on over $3 billion of bank loans, and the lenders seized the Plaza Hotel along with most of his other properties in a 'vast and humiliating restructuring' that allowed him to avoid personal bankruptcy. Trump founded Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR), which assumed ownership of the Trump Plaza.
Donald Trump took the Miss Universe and Miss USA pageants to NBC due to disagreements with CBS about scheduling.
Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR) went bankrupt, leaving Trump with 10% ownership.
Donald Trump cofounded Trump University, a company that sold real estate seminars for up to $35,000.
Donald Trump's hotel and casino businesses in Atlantic City and New York filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection for the sixth time.
Trump University changed its name to the Trump Entrepreneur Initiative after New York State authorities notified the company that its use of 'university' violated state law.
The State of New York filed a $40 million civil suit against Trump University, alleging that the company made false statements and defrauded consumers.
Donald Trump promised to release his tax returns if he ran for office.

Donald Trump again promised to release his tax returns if he ran for office. Trump's leadership style and political agenda, often referred to as Trumpism, have reshaped the Republican Party's identity and have been characterized as racist or misogynistic.

Donald Trump announced his candidacy for the 2016 presidential election, campaigning as a rich, successful businessman and an outsider without political experience, and claimed media bias against him. Donald Trump claimed Mexican immigrants were 'bringing drugs, they're bringing crime, they're rapists' in his campaign launch speech.
NBC and Univision dropped the Miss Universe pageants in reaction to Donald Trump's comments about Mexican immigrants.
Donald Trump did not release his tax returns, contrary to his promises and the practice of every major candidate since 1976.
Donald Trump imposed a travel ban on seven Muslim-majority countries. Donald Trump expanded the Mexico–United States border wall. Donald Trump enforced a family separation policy on the border.
Donald Trump had been involved in more than 4,000 lawsuits, liens, and other filings, often filed for nonpayment against him by employees, contractors, real estate brokers, and his own attorneys.
Trump was impeached for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress.
Trump downplayed the severity of the COVID-19 pandemic, contradicted health officials, and signed the CARES Act in response to the pandemic.
Trump was impeached for incitement of insurrection.
Trump's attempt to overturn the 2020 presidential election result culminated in the January 6 Capitol attack.
Trump was found liable in civil cases for sexual abuse and defamation and for business fraud.

Trump was found guilty in 34 counts of falsifying business records, making him the first U.S. president convicted of a felony. Trump initiated mass layoffs of federal workers at the start of his second presidency. Trump imposed tariffs on nearly all countries at the highest level since the Great Depression and signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. The Internal Revenue Service was investigating whether Trump had twice written off losses incurred through construction cost overruns and lagging sales of residential units in the Trump International Hotel and Tower in Chicago.
Trump authorized a joint U.S./Israeli war with Iran that resulted in the killing of Iran's leader.
Donald Trump made 'explicitly racist and sexist appeals to win over white voters' during his 2016 presidential campaign.
Donald Trump pledged to build a wall on the Mexico-U.S. border and vowed that Mexico would pay for it.
Donald Trump promised to deport millions of illegal immigrants residing in the U.S.
Donald Trump criticized birthright citizenship for incentivizing 'anchor babies'.
Donald Trump agreed to pay a total of $25 million to settle three cases related to Trump University.
Donald Trump described NATO as 'obsolete' and espoused views described by The Washington Post as noninterventionist and protectionist.
Donald Trump's campaign platform emphasized renegotiating U.S.-China relations and free trade agreements such as NAFTA and strongly enforcing immigration laws.
Portions of Donald Trump's state filings for 1995 were leaked, showing he had declared a loss of $916 million that year, which could have let him avoid taxes for up to 18 years.
The New York attorney general's office ordered the Donald J. Trump Foundation to cease its fundraising activities in New York immediately due to violations of state law.
The Washington Post reported that the Donald J. Trump Foundation had committed several potential legal and ethical violations, including self-dealing and tax evasion.
Donald Trump continued to profit from his businesses during his presidency, despite potential conflicts of interest.
Donald Trump's administration pursued policies that benefited his businesses, including the Trump Organization's operational expansions in Scotland, Dubai, and the Dominican Republic.
Lobbyists, foreign government officials, and Trump donors and allies generated hundreds of millions of dollars for his resorts and hotels.
Donald Trump reduced the budget for renewable energy research by 40 percent and reversed Obama-era policies directed at curbing climate change.
Donald Trump withdrew from the Paris Agreement, making the U.S. the only nation to not ratify it.
Donald Trump rolled back more than 100 federal environmental regulations, including those that curbed greenhouse gas emissions, air and water pollution, and the use of toxic substances.

Trump dismantled federal regulations on health, labor, the environment, and other areas, including a bill that revoked the Obama-era regulation restricting the sale of firearms to severely mentally ill people.
Trump delayed, suspended, or reversed ninety federal regulations, often after requests by the regulated industries.
Trump vowed to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act.
Donald Trump took the oath of office as President, despite having no prior government or military experience.
Donald Trump signed executive orders, including authorizing procedures for repealing the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act and planning for a wall along the U.S. border with Mexico.
Trump expressed a desire to 'let Obamacare fail'; his administration halved the enrollment period and drastically reduced funding for enrollment promotion.
Trump signed an executive order to scale back the implementation of the Affordable Care Act.
Donald Trump signed the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, which reduced tax rates for businesses and individuals and eliminated the penalty associated with the Affordable Care Act's individual mandate.
The New York attorney general's office filed a civil suit against the Donald J. Trump Foundation, seeking $2.8 million in restitution and additional penalties.
The Donald J. Trump Foundation ceased operation and disbursed its assets to other charities.
The federal budget deficit increased by almost 50 percent under Donald Trump, to nearly $1 trillion.
A New York state judge ordered Donald Trump to pay $2 million to a group of charities for misusing the foundation's funds, in part to finance his presidential campaign.
The U.S. national debt increased by 39 percent under Donald Trump, reaching $27.75 trillion, and the U.S. debt-to-GDP ratio hit a post-World War II high.
The U.S. Supreme Court allowed Donald Trump's tax returns and other records to be released to the Manhattan district attorney for a criminal investigation.
Trump pledged that Roe v. Wade would be overturned 'automatically' if he were elected and given the opportunity to appoint two or three anti-abortion justices.
The FBI launched Crossfire Hurricane, an investigation into possible links between Russia and Trump's 2016 campaign.
Trump barred organizations that provide abortions or abortion referrals from receiving federal funds.
Trump described illegal immigration as an 'invasion' of the United States and drastically escalated immigration enforcement.
Trump increased restrictions on granting permanent residency to immigrants needing public benefits.
Trump built 73 miles of wall in areas without barriers and 365 miles to replace older barriers along the U.S.-Mexico border.
Trump implemented a travel ban targeting predominantly Muslim countries, which was blocked by courts but later upheld by the Supreme Court in 2019.
Trump disparaged courts and judges he disagreed with, often in personal terms, and questioned the judiciary's constitutional authority.
Three U.S. intelligence agencies jointly stated with 'high confidence' that Russia interfered in the 2016 presidential election to favor Trump.
Trump signed an executive order that denied entry to citizens from six Muslim-majority countries for four months and from Syria indefinitely.
Trump dismissed Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) director James Comey, saying a few days later that he was concerned about Comey's role in the Trump-Russia investigations.
The FBI opened a second investigation into Trump's personal and business dealings with Russia after he fired Comey.
Trump replaced the initial travel ban with a presidential proclamation extending travel bans to North Koreans, Chadians, and some Venezuelan officials.
Trump said many of the sub-cabinet positions in the executive branch are unnecessary, resulting in hundreds of positions without a nominee.
The Supreme Court allowed Trump's revised travel ban to go into effect.
Trump signed legislation to increase funding for drug treatments in response to the opioid epidemic, but was widely criticized for failing to make a concrete strategy.
The Trump administration had a policy of family separation that separated over 4,400 children from migrant parents at the U.S.-Mexico border.
Trump mandated that illegal immigrant families be detained together unless there was a concern for the child's risk, following public outrage over the family separation policy.
A judge ordered that the families separated at the border be reunited and further separations stopped except in limited circumstances.
Trump began a trade war with China, imposing tariffs and trade barriers in an effort to force China to end unfair trade practices and intellectual property infringement.
Trump's refusal to sign any spending bill unless it allocated funding for the border wall resulted in the longest-ever federal government shutdown, for 35 days from December 2018 to January 2019.
The Supreme Court ultimately upheld Trump's travel ban.
Of 706 key positions, 433 had been filled and Trump had no nominee for 264.
Trump is a long-time advocate of capital punishment, and his administration oversaw the federal government execute 13 prisoners, more than in the previous 56 years combined.
Trump's administration took an anti-marijuana position, revoking Obama-era policies that provided protections for states that legalized marijuana.
Trump's administration rolled back key components of the Obama administration's workplace protections against discrimination of LGBTQ people.
Trump's attempted rollback of anti-discrimination protections for transgender patients was halted by a federal judge.
Trump granted 237 requests for clemency, fewer than all presidents since 1900 with the exception of George H. W. Bush and George W. Bush.
Trump reduced the number of refugees admitted to record lows, from an annual limit of 110,000 before he took office to 15,000 in 2021.
Trump granted 73 pardons and commuted 70 sentences in his last full day in office.
Journalist E. Jean Carroll accused Trump of raping her in the 1990s and sued him for defamation over his denial
Mueller's final report found that Russia interfered in 2016 to favor Trump and that Trump and his campaign welcomed and encouraged the effort
The Mueller report detailed potential obstruction of justice by Trump
Trump pressured Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy to investigate Joe and Hunter Biden
Trump withdrew the U.S. from the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty, citing alleged Russian noncompliance.
The House voted to impeach Trump for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress
Trump initially ignored public health warnings and calls for action from health officials within his administration during the COVID-19 pandemic.
Trump described himself as a 'nationalist' and his foreign policy as 'America First', supporting populist, neo-nationalist, and authoritarian governments.
Trump established the White House Coronavirus Task Force.
The Senate acquitted Trump of abuse of power and obstruction of Congress
Trump signed into law the CARES Act, a $2.2 trillion bipartisan economic stimulus bill, the largest stimulus in U.S. history.
Trump halted funding of the World Health Organization.
Trump encouraged right-wing protests against social-distancing policies in states with stay-at-home orders.
Trump repeatedly refused to say whether he would accept the results of the presidential election if he lost
Trump repeatedly pressured federal health agencies to take actions he favored, such as approving unproven treatments.
Trump pressured federal health agencies to approve unproven treatments for COVID-19.
Trump declared victory in the presidential election before the results were known
Trump baselessly alleged election fraud after Joe Biden was projected the winner
Trump and his allies filed many lawsuits challenging the election results, which were rejected by at least 86 judges
Trump held a rally at which he called for the election to be overturned and urged his supporters to 'fight like hell' and 'take back our country'
Trump's supporters formed a mob that broke into the Capitol building, disrupting certification and causing the evacuation of Congress
The House charged Trump with incitement of insurrection for his role in the January 6 Capitol attack
Donald Trump began using the term 'big lie' to refer to the 2020 election itself, despite his critics using it to describe his false claims about the election
The Republican Party used Trump's false claims about the election to justify imposing new voting restrictions in its favor
Carroll sued Trump again for battery and further defamation
Trump was found liable for sexual abuse and defamation and ordered to pay $5 million in one case and $83.3 million in the other
New York filed a civil lawsuit against Trump accusing him of inflating the Trump Organization's value to gain an advantage with lenders and banks
Trump was found liable and ordered to pay nearly $355 million plus interest in the New York civil lawsuit
Trump took credit when Roe was overturned by Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization in 2022; all three of his Supreme Court nominees voted with the majority.
Trump launched a pro-Trump social media platform called Truth Social

The U.S. House committee on the January 6 attack recommended criminal charges against Trump for obstructing an official proceeding, inciting or assisting an insurrection, and conspiracy to defraud the United States
Trump's campaign began diverting 10% of donations to his leadership PAC
Trump's campaign paid $100 million towards his legal bills by March 2024
A federal grand jury indicted Trump on 31 counts of 'willfully retaining national defense information' under the Espionage Act, among other charges
A grand jury in Fulton County, Georgia, indicted Trump on 13 charges, including racketeering, for his efforts to subvert the 2020 election in the state
Trump made increasingly violent and authoritarian statements during his campaign
Trump said he would weaponize the FBI and the Justice Department against his political opponents
Trump used harsher and more dehumanizing anti-immigrant rhetoric than during his presidency
Trump's rhetoric was described by some historians and scholars as authoritarian, fascist, and unlike anything a political candidate has ever said in American history

Trump was convicted on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records, stemming from evidence that he booked Michael Cohen's hush-money payments to adult film actress Stormy Daniels as business expenses
Trump was shot in the ear in an assassination attempt at a campaign rally in Butler Township, Pennsylvania
Trump mentioned 'rigged election' and 'election interference' earlier and more frequently than in the 2016 and 2020 campaigns and refused to commit to accepting the 2024 election results

Donald Trump's administration asserted a constitutional right to ignore federal law in its justification of several actions, including refusing to enforce a federal ban on TikTok, with legal experts describing it as claiming a 'constitutional power to immunize private parties to commit otherwise illegal acts with impunity'

Trump's second presidency has been described as having fewer guardrails against conflicts of interest than his first, breaking with decades of ethical norms and raising substantial corruption concerns

Trump maintained a publicly traded company in Trump Media & Technology Group and diversified it into financial services, while also pursuing new overseas real estate deals involving state-affiliated entities and having several branding and licensing deals selling Trump-branded merchandise
Trump became the oldest to assume the presidency and the first president with a felony conviction
Trump issued more executive orders than any other U.S. president's first 100 days
Trump profited from events held at his hotels and golf courses and did not place his assets in a blind trust, as previous presidents had done
Trump launched, promoted, and personally benefited from two cryptocurrency tokens ('meme coins'), $Trump and $Melania
Trump directly benefited from his family's cryptocurrency company World Liberty Financial, which engaged in an unprecedented mixing of private enterprise and government policy
Trump implemented a hiring freeze across the federal government and ordered telework of federal employees to be discontinued within 30 days
Trump ordered a review of many career civil service positions with the intention of reclassifying them into at-will positions without job protections

Trump initiated mass job terminations of federal employees, which were described by legal experts as unprecedented or in violation of federal law, with the intent of replacing them with workers more aligned with his agenda
By late February 2025, the Trump administration had fired more than 30,000 federal employees

Trump ordered an end to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) projects in the federal government and rescinded Lyndon B. Johnson's 1965 Executive Order 11246, which had mandated that federal contractors take affirmative action to end racial discrimination

Trump and Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency largely dismantled several federal agencies, including USAID and the Department of Education, unilaterally fired several thousand staff, and reduced administrative functions to statutory minimums

Trump took a series of actions using the government to target political opponents and civil society, including threatening, signing executive actions, and ordering investigations into his political opponents, critics, and organizations aligned with the Democratic Party
Trump ended the post-Watergate norm of Justice Department independence, weaponizing it and agencies across the federal government to target his political enemies

Trump's actions against civil society were described by hundreds of legal experts and political scientists as authoritarian, contributing to democratic backsliding, and negatively impacting the rule of law
Trump defied judges and the courts in roughly one third of all cases against him, actions which were described by legal experts as unprecedented for any presidential administration

Trump ran full-page newspaper advertisements and insisted that a group of five black and Latino teenagers were guilty of raping a white woman in the Central Park jogger case, even after they were cleared of suspicion in 2002.

Donald Trump bragged about kissing and groping women without their consent in a 2005 'hot mic' recording, saying that 'when you're a star, they let you do it. You can do anything. ... Grab 'em by the pussy'

Trump has a history of belittling women when speaking to the media and on social media, making lewd comments, disparaging women's physical appearances, and referring to them using derogatory epithets.
Trump's comments and actions were characterized as racist and helped bring once-fringe far-right ideas and organizations into the mainstream.
Trump's rhetoric has been described as using fearmongering and demagogy, which intensified during his 2024 presidential campaign.
Trump's politics and rhetoric led to the formation of a political movement known as Trumpism, which has been compared to a cult of personality.
Donald Trump characterized his comments about kissing and groping women without their consent as 'locker-room talk'
Donald Trump issued his first public apology for the 'hot mic' recording during his 2016 presidential campaign

Trump's comments and actions have been characterized as racist, including his remarks on the 2017 Unite the Right rally, which implied a moral equivalence between white supremacist demonstrators and counter-protesters.
Trump referred to El Salvador, Haiti, Honduras, and African nations as 'shithole countries', remarks condemned as racist.
A House of Representatives resolution condemned Trump for racist remarks about four minority Democratic congresswomen.

At least 26 women have publicly accused Donald Trump of sexual misconduct, including rape, kissing without consent, groping, looking under women's skirts, and walking in on naked teenage pageant contestants
Trump's pardons and grants of clemency favored political allies and loyalists, and disproportionately pardoned 'the powerful, famous, well-connected and wealthy' accused of white-collar crime.

Trump granted clemency to all January 6 rioters convicted or charged on his first day in office, including those who violently attacked police, by pardoning more than 1,500 and commuting the sentences of 14.
Trump ordered agencies to stop enforcement of disfavored rules in an attempt at large-scale deregulation that legal experts described as illegal and contrary to decades of federal law.

Trump sought greater government control over private businesses and shifted away from traditional conservative free market orthodoxy, engaging in state capitalism by taking direct government equity stakes in multiple U.S. corporations.

Trump canceled and paused federal grants and made large cuts to scientific research, several of which were found by judges and the Government Accountability Office as being illegal and unconstitutional.
Trump and his administration's Make America Healthy Again agenda promoted anti-science and anti-vaccine activism, resulting in a resurgence of whooping cough and measles.
Trump relied on declaring national emergencies to justify hundreds of actions and bypass congressional approval or regulatory review.
Trump declared a national energy emergency, allowing the suspension of environmental regulations, loosening the rules for fossil fuel extraction and limiting renewable energy projects.
Trump withdrew the U.S. from the Paris Agreement on climate change during his second presidency.
Trump attributed societal problems to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) initiatives and wokeness, equating diversity with incompetence.
Trump reversed pro-diversity policies in the federal government.
Trump threatened cultural institutions and sixty universities on DEI and antisemitism grounds.
Trump forced law firms to capitulate to his political agenda.
Trump expanded the domestic use of the military and ordered military deployments to several Democratic Party-led cities.
Trump banned transgender people from the military.

Trump restricted or defunded gender-affirming healthcare, opposed inclusive language, censored research and education materials, targeted schools, universities, and cultural institutions accused of promoting what his government calls 'gender ideology', barred transgender athletes from sports, and required U.S. passports to state transgender people's sex assigned at birth.
Trump instructed Border Patrol agents to summarily deport illegal immigrants crossing the border and disabled the CBP One app that was being used to schedule border crossings.

Trump signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act into law, making the temporary tax cuts of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act permanent and adding additional tax deductions for a total of around $4.5 trillion, mostly benefiting the highest income brackets and costing people in the lowest income bracket $1,600 per year.
Trump posed as a Trump Organization official named 'John Barron' to influence his ranking on the Forbes 400 list of wealthy Americans.
Trump self-reported his net worth as minus $900 million.
The Taj Mahal casino, owned by Donald Trump, emerged from bankruptcy.
Trump's Castle and Plaza filed for bankruptcy.
Donald Trump became the leading proponent of the racist 'birther' conspiracy theory that Barack Obama, the first black U.S. president, was not born in the United States

Trump's campaign released a letter from his longtime personal physician, Harold Bornstein, stating that he would 'be the healthiest individual ever elected to the presidency', which was later revealed to be dictated by Trump himself.
Donald Trump promoted conspiracy theories such as climate change denial, alleged Ukrainian interference in U.S. elections, and vaccines and autism.
An investigation found that Donald Trump's charity, the Trump Foundation, had been spending other people's money.
The Trump Foundation was ordered to stop fundraising by the New York attorney general's office.
Donald Trump agreed to pay $25 million in a settlement related to Trump University.
Donald Trump announced that he would dissolve his charitable foundation after mounting complaints.
Donald Trump made over 30,573 false or misleading statements during his first presidency, according to The Washington Post
Donald Trump's rhetoric has been associated with an increased incidence of hate crimes
Donald Trump has been identified as a key figure in increasing political violence in the U.S., both for and against him

Federal law-enforcement officials used tear gas and other crowd control tactics to remove a largely peaceful crowd of lawful protesters from Lafayette Square, outside the White House, and Trump then posed with a Bible for a photo op at the nearby St. John's Episcopal Church.
Trump's 2024 campaign made extensive use of dehumanizing language and racial stereotypes.
Trump resumed the Remain in Mexico policy, designated drug cartels as terrorist groups, and ordered construction to be resumed on a border wall.
Trump revoked the parole status of immigrants who entered the U.S. under CBP One and ended humanitarian parole for Cubans, Haitians, Nicaraguans.
Trump targeted activists, legal immigrants, tourists, and students with visas who expressed criticism of his policies or pro-Palestinian advocacy.
Several American citizens were detained and deported.
Trump imposed tariffs on most countries, including large tariffs on major trading partners China, Canada, and Mexico.
Trump selected cabinet members with personal loyalty to him, with the focus on loyalty over subject-matter expertise.
Trump and Vice President Vance berated Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy in a highly contentious televised meeting.
Trump used the Alien Enemies Act of 1798 to imprison illegal immigrants without trial at the Terrorism Confinement Center in El Salvador.
Trump ordered the bombing of Iranian nuclear sites.
Trump's plan for a Gaza ceasefire deal between Israel and Hamas was signed, leading to the creation of the Board of Peace.
Trump pardoned former Honduran president Juan Orlando Hernández, who had been extradited to the U.S. in 2022 and sentenced to 45 years in prison for drug trafficking.
Trump's aggressive and hardline deportation campaign led to large nationwide protests and violent confrontations with migrants and protesters.
The Supreme Court ruled the tariffs Trump imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to be illegal.
Trump authorized joint U.S./Israeli air strikes on Iran.
Trump issued an executive order scaling back parts of Obamacare
Donald Trump issued a travel ban that caused chaos and protests at US airports, affecting immigration and refugees
Trump's agents seized his medical records from his longtime personal physician, Harold Bornstein, in a raid on Bornstein's office.
A federal judge temporarily halted Trump's order on immigration and refugees
Trump rolled back an Obama regulation making it easier for the mentally ill to get guns
Trump rolled back regulations on Wall Street, gun sellers, and polluters
Trump signed a new travel ban directive
A Hawaii judge halted Trump's new travel ban before it could go into effect
LGBTQ advocates said Trump's new executive order made them vulnerable to discrimination
Donald Trump had potential conflicts of interest due to his business dealings
The Supreme Court let Trump's latest travel ban go into partial effect
Trump said he planned to 'let Obamacare fail'
Trump cut funding for Obamacare enrollment programs
Trump extended his travel ban to include blocks on North Korea, Venezuela, and Chad
Trump signed an executive order aimed at undermining Obamacare
The Trump Foundation agreed to dissolve, accused of a 'shocking pattern of illegality'.
Donald Trump was ordered to pay $2 million to a collection of non-profits as part of a civil lawsuit.
At least 54 criminal cases, from August 2015 to April 2020, invoked Donald Trump in direct connection with violence or threats of violence, mostly by white men and primarily against minorities
Donald Trump's promotion of antimalarial drugs as a treatment for COVID-19 contributed to a U.S. shortage of these drugs and panic buying in Africa and South Asia
Donald Trump frequently accused the press of bias, calling it the 'fake news media' and 'the enemy of the people'.
Twitter began to tag some of Donald Trump's tweets with fact-checks.
Donald Trump refused to condemn the white supremacist Proud Boys during a 2020 presidential debate
Twitter banned Donald Trump after the January 6 attack.
Donald Trump was banned from Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, and other platforms after the January 6 attack.

Donald Trump repeatedly voiced support for outlawing political dissent and criticism, and said that reporters should be prosecuted for not divulging confidential sources and media companies should possibly lose their broadcast licenses for unfavorable coverage of him.
The Federal Communications Commission launched investigations into media outlets accused of bias against Donald Trump.
The American Political Science Association ranked Trump the worst president.
Trump's administration did not release files relating to Jeffrey Epstein, despite Trump's promise to do so during the 2024 campaign.
Some of the files related to Jeffrey Epstein were released, mostly heavily redacted.
Roughly three million additional pages related to Jeffrey Epstein were released, including approximately 38,000 references to Trump, his wife, Mar-a-Lago, and other Trump-related terms.
President Trump contradicted the official White House account of the Comey firing
Trump pressured Comey to 'lift the cloud' of inquiry
The Trump administration announced the US would withdraw from the Paris climate agreement
The Supreme Court let Trump's latest travel ban go into full effect
The Trump administration's withdrawal from the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty (INF) was completed, sparking concerns about the impact on global security.
The Trump White House had the highest turnover in 40 years, with many high-level officials leaving or being fired during his presidency.
The Trump administration implemented a 'zero tolerance' policy at the border, resulting in the separation of over 5,400 children from their families.
Trump reportedly claimed that Crimea is part of Russia because everyone there speaks Russian, sparking controversy and criticism.
Despite claims that a GOP immigration bill would end family separation, experts said it would not, and the Trump administration continued to blame Democrats for the policy.
Trump signed an executive order aimed at ending family separation, but thousands of children may have remained apart from their families.
The Supreme Court upheld Trump's travel ban
A judge ordered the US government to reunite families separated at the border within 30 days, criticizing the government's handling of the situation.
Trump publicly identified as a nationalist, sparking uproar and controversy.
Trump and Democrats failed to reach an agreement to reopen the government, leading to the longest shutdown in US history
Trump signed a bill to temporarily reopen the government after the longest shutdown in US history
Trump declared a national emergency to build a border wall, setting up a massive legal fight
The Trump administration doubled down on its anti-marijuana position
Trump proposed cuts to climate and clean-energy programs
Trump stepped into North Korea and agreed with Kim Jong-un to resume talks, despite concerns about the lack of progress on denuclearization.
The Trump administration escalated tensions with Europe as a crisis loomed, with Trump's 'America First' policy being criticized as becoming 'Trump First'.
Trump returned to supporting the death penalty as Democrats turned against it
Trump used words like 'invasion' and 'killer' at rallies more than 500 times since 2017, according to a USA Today analysis
Trump's policies on abortion were compared to Biden's, with Trump's being more restrictive
Trump virtually cut off refugees, unleashing a tirade on immigrants
The Trump administration ended the US role as a worldwide leader on refugees
The Trump administration was accused of sidelining scientists and their research
The Trump administration doubled down on its anti-marijuana position
A judge halted Trump's rollback of transgender health protections
Trump spurned science on climate change, saying 'don't think science knows'
Critics said Trump fumbled the response to the opioid crisis
The Trump administration's policies and actions raised concerns about the survival of US democracy, with some experts questioning whether it could survive Trump's presidency.
Donald Trump cut refugee admissions to a record low
Trump created a task force to lead the US coronavirus response
A crackdown on immigrants who use public benefits took effect
Trump halted funding to the World Health Organization
Trump encouraged protests against governors who imposed virus restrictions
The FDA pulled emergency approval for antimalarial drugs touted by Trump as a COVID-19 treatment
Trump pressed for plasma therapy and officials worried about an unvetted vaccine
Trump wielded pardon power as a political weapon, rewarding loyalists and undermining prosecutors
The Trump administration carried out its 13th and final execution
Trump's administration rushed to carry out federal executions in his final days in office
Donald Trump granted clemency to 144 people in his final hours in office
Trump took credit for the Dobbs decision which overturned Roe v. Wade
Trump said 'I was able to kill Roe v. Wade'
Trump signed executive order directing US withdrawal from the Paris climate agreement
Trump signed sweeping actions on immigration and border security
Federal workers were ordered to report on colleagues over diversity, equity, and inclusion crackdown
Trump proposed a plan to 'clean out' Gaza and suggested that Jordan and Egypt should accept more Palestinian refugees
Trump's energy emergency declaration was seen as a gift to fossil fuel firms
Trump's sweeping immigration crackdown targeted some legal means to enter the US
Trump attacked diversity, and a racist undercurrent surfaced
Trump's executive actions curbing transgender rights focused on 'gender ideology'
Trump expressed indifference or hostility towards European allies, raising alarm
Trump and his team erupted at Ukrainian President Zelensky in a tense Oval Office meeting
Trump continued to hollow out the federal workforce, having already fired more than 30,000 employees
Trump's clash with Zelensky marked a defining turn away from US defense of democracies
Trump's tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China took effect
Experts said Trump's targeting of law firms was unprecedented
El Salvador's mega-prison held Venezuelans deported from the US
Trump's science policies posed long-term risk, according to economists
Donald Trump raised birther questions about President Obama
Donald Trump entertained conspiracy theories
A Harvard study criticized the media's role in Donald Trump's rise
Donald Trump made unprecedented false statements for a modern presidential candidate, according to the Los Angeles Times.
Donald Trump made false claims about the size of his inaugural crowd, according to PolitiFact.
Donald Trump's lies were analyzed in an article by Politico Magazine, which explored how his lies affect the brain.
Donald Trump made false allegations against Obama, including 'treason'
Donald Trump's frequency of lying increased over time, according to an article in The New Yorker.
News media hesitated to use 'lie' for Trump's misstatements
Donald Trump rarely apologized for his actions as President
Donald Trump had made over 5,000 false statements as president by March 2019, according to the Toronto Star.
Donald Trump's history of conspiracy theories was documented
Donald Trump promoted hydroxychloroquine as a treatment for COVID-19 despite a lack of evidence, according to The Guardian.
The news media started to describe Trump's 'falsehoods' as lies
Trump's attacks on the press showed 'ominous signs' for the American press
Trump suggested deploying more troops in cities, bending the military's role
A federal judge deemed Trump administration's termination of NIH grants illegal
Trump signed a tax and spending bill that included tax cuts and potential future benefit cuts
Trump walked away from the UN Cultural Agency as China flexed its muscles
